summary: McKenzie describes his market selection process for Kalzumeus, his second software product. He identifies a niche in the investment space where small businesses face high-value problems that existing solutions address poorly due to prohibitive startup costs. He plans to build a web application targeting these underserved small fish with simple pricing and lower barriers to entry. His strategic thinking reveals a deliberate shift from hobby software (Bingo Card Creator) toward building a business that could eventually replace full-time employment.
key_points: The best pricing justification comes from saving customers multiples of what you charge — saving $2000 makes a $250 price easy to justify
Fragmented markets with many small competitors are ideal for micro-ISVs because no single player dominates
When potential customers are actively complaining about their problems on forums, that's a strong signal of unmet demand
Simple, transparent pricing can be a competitive advantage when incumbents make customers do math to figure out costs
Targeting customers that incumbents consider too small to bother with creates a viable niche with low competitive pressure
money_quotes: It is far easier to justify someone paying you $250 if you save them $2000 than it is to justify someone paying you $24.95 if you save them $10-15 a few times a year.
Many of the solutions involve calculations on paper, elbow grease, and very expensive professionals doing things that could be done by a trained monkey with a slide rule.

Small businesses or individuals not using these services, instead preferring to fix the problem by themselves, and then complaining about it to anyone who will listen on their forums. Ahh, music to my ears.
Fragmented markets are the uISV's best friend.
There is something about replacing paper and slide-rule totting monkeys with computers that suggests a plausible increase in process efficiency.
The simplicity of 'We have ONE price for ALL customers and are interested in YOUR business even if our competitors think you're too small to be bothered with' might be a decent differentiator.
I could cover my burn rate with about 200 customers and will replace my next job's salary well before hitting a thousand.
key_insight: The most promising micro-ISV opportunities exist where incumbents have high fixed costs that exclude small customers, creating an underserved niche that actively complains about unsolved problems.
